figures.caption: Figure 2: Example of one-track LEG with a single specialist agent.
Figure 3: Example of one-track LEG with multiple specialist agents.

Table 1: End-to-end evaluation of the graph-mode multi-agent system across six query-complexity categories (N=37). Factual Precision measures closeness of system-computed values to official reference values from NHC Tropical Cyclone Reports and NOAA CO-OPS Technical Reports (continuous, 1− |ŷ − y|/y). Topology Selection evaluates whether the architect correctly chose linear vs. parallel_tracks. Agent F1 is the harmonic mean of precision and recall for specialist selection. Source Attribution scores citation of station IDs, vertical datums, temporal references, and authoritative source names across the full processing pipeline (consolidator, cross-track merge, and reporter outputs). All values in %. “–” indicates the metric is not applicable to that category. Model: Claude Sonnet 4.6.

Table 4: Per-query token usage and API cost on the representative subset (Table A.4). Tokens are summed across all pipeline stages (architect, specialists, consolidator, reporter). Cost computed at published Claude Sonnet 4.6 rates.
Table A.3: Parallel-track queries (n = 12). “2T” and “3T” denote the expected number of independent parallel tracks.

Table 3: Stress tests evaluating robustness across four dimensions. (a) Query complexity scaling from 1 to 5 independent sub-questions. (b) Paraphrase robustness: each query rephrased two additional ways (3× per group). (c) Adversarial robustness: five queries containing factual errors, impossible scenarios, or misleading premises. (d) Fault tolerance: simulated outage of one federal data source at a time. Model: Claude Sonnet 4.6.
Table A.5: Paraphrase variants. Variant (a) is the original benchmark phrasing; (b) and (c) test casual and terse registers.
Table A.2: Linear multi-agent queries (n = 13). “NHC→NOAA” denotes a two-layer LEG; “NHC→N+U” denotes NHC followed by parallel NOAA CO-OPS+USGS.
